hook: You already know how to measure distances and angles in $\mathbb{R}^2$ and $\mathbb{R}^3$ using the dot product — but what if you need to measure 'distance' between functions, signals, or high-dimensional data? Standard vector operations don't tell the whole story. This unit reveals how a single powerful structure, the inner product, lets you do geometry in almost any vector space.
concept: The unifying idea is that by defining an inner product $\langle u, v \rangle$ on a vector space, you unlock the full language of geometry — lengths, angles, and perpendicularity — which in turn enables powerful tools like optimal approximation and signal decomposition.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to define and work with inner products and norms on general vector spaces, extending familiar dot-product geometry beyond $\mathbb{R}^n$.
Master the concept of orthogonality and discover why orthonormal bases make coordinate computations dramatically simpler via $\langle v, e_i \rangle$.
Apply the Gram-Schmidt algorithm to systematically transform any basis into an orthonormal one, building a foundational computational skill used throughout applied math.
Use orthogonal projection to find the closest vector in a subspace to any given vector, solving real-world least squares problems when a system $Ax = b$ has no exact solution.
Derive the $QR$ factorization of a matrix by formalizing Gram-Schmidt, and see how it provides a numerically stable method for solving least squares problems efficiently.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Fourier Series as an Inner Product Space Application (H)
topic_roadmap.description: Apply inner product space theory to function spaces, understanding Fourier series as orthogonal projections onto trigonometric bases — a stunning payoff for everything you've built in this unit.
